sections.text: Welcome back to AP Computer Science A. Module Eight — two-dimensional arrays. Today we move from a single row of data to a full grid. By the end of this lesson you'll be reading, writing, and traversing 2D arrays like a chess board.

Think about a chess board. Eight rows, eight columns. Every square has an address — a row and a column. Now think about a digital image — rows of pixels by columns of pixels. Or a spreadsheet — rows and columns. Anywhere data is grid-shaped, you reach for a 2D array.

Three things to cover. One — how to declare a 2D array and index into it. Two — how to traverse it, row by row, with nested for loops. Three — real applications, and the single most common trap: confusing rows and columns.

Two ways to declare a 2D array. First, new keyword — int bracket bracket grid equals new int three four. That gives you three rows by four columns, all zeros. Second, literal — int bracket bracket g equals brace brace one two brace brace three four brace brace five six. Three rows, two columns, values baked in. Same shape, different syntax.

Here's the key idea. A 2D array is really an array of arrays. So grid dot length gives you the number of rows — that's the outer length. And grid bracket zero dot length gives you the number of columns — that's the inner length, the length of one row. Memorize these two.

To access an element you write the array name, then bracket row, bracket column. g bracket zero bracket zero is the top-left corner. g bracket one bracket zero is the second row, first column. g bracket zero bracket one is the first row, second column. Row first, then column. Always.

The standard traversal pattern. Nested for loops. Outer loop counts rows from zero up to g dot length. Inner loop counts columns from zero up to g zero dot length. Inside the body you touch g of r c. This is called row-major order — you walk across each row before moving to the next.

Your turn. You have a three by three integer grid. Write nested for loops that sum all nine elements. Hint — outer loop runs rows zero through two, inner loop runs columns zero through two. Pause now, write the code, press play when you're ready.

Time. Start with int sum equals zero. Outer for r equals zero, r less than three, r plus plus. Inner for c equals zero, c less than three, c plus plus. Body — sum plus equals grid r c. Done. Nine cells visited, all added into sum.

Now flip the loops. Outer counts columns, inner counts rows. Same access — g bracket r bracket c — but now you walk down each column before moving to the next column. This is column-major order. Less common, but the AP exam will absolutely ask you to trace it.

There's a cleaner option when you don't need indices. A nested enhanced-for. Outer loop pulls out each row as an int array. Inner loop pulls out each value from that row. Two for-each loops, no counter variables, no off-by-one errors. Use it whenever you only need the values, not their positions.

Three real applications. Tic-tac-toe — a three-by-three char array of X, O, and blank. A gradebook — students down the rows, tests across the columns. A digital image — every pixel is one cell of a 2D array of color values. The same nested-for pattern handles all of them.

Here's the trap. The single most common 2D array bug. Writing g bracket col bracket row instead of g bracket row bracket col. The order matters. Always row first, then column. Picture it — the first index moves you down the grid, the second index moves you across. Down, then across.

Recap. A 2D array is an array of arrays. Index is always row first, column second. Grid dot length gives rows, grid zero dot length gives columns. Nested for loops to traverse. Row-major is standard. Tic-tac-toe, gradebooks, and images are all 2D arrays. Your assignment is in the dashboard.

This video is the overview. To actually master 2D arrays, here's the rest. Work the 2D Array chapter on Runestone — it has interactive code traces. Practice on Codingbat AP-1 with the 2D problems. Submit the assignment in your dashboard. Book a check-in with your advisor if column-major traversal still feels fuzzy. See you in Module Nine — Inheritance.
